item.title: 9 reasons every PC enthusiast needs a 3D printer
item.itunes:title: 9 reasons every PC enthusiast needs a 3D printer
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Hey, PC enthusiasts, have you ever considered using 3D printing to customize your PC? It’s not just for modding your gaming rig; it can also help with maintenance and upgrades. 3D printing lets you create custom parts and accessories on the fly, experiment with new case designs, and even customize your whole system to suit your unique needs.

If you’re into tinkering with hardware or just want to make your workspace more personalized, a 3D printer can be a game-changer. Here are nine reasons to consider getting one for your PC building projects.

 Related
You can now build your own 3D printer for less with this amazing open-source project

Never be stuck looking for a specific part again.

1

9 Connect with a sharing community

Find designs online and contribute your own ideas

Online repositories like Thingiverse and Printables are a treasure trove of free models for PC components and accessories. You can easily download mounts, replacement parts, or even decorative items and customize them to fit your specific build. This is especially helpful for people who want a quick solution or are still learning to use 3D modeling software.

Sharing your creations also builds a supportive and fun community among hobbyists. You can upload your custom parts, ask for feedback, and learn from experienced people. This exchange of ideas can lead to new and better ways to 3D print and build PCs.

Many people share tips on how to make your prints better and how to modify your PC. They often give advice on things like slicer settings and how to mount your GPU. By exploring these resources, you can find simple solutions to problems that are hard to solve when 3D printing.

8 Experiment with different 3D filaments

Explore advanced materials for more robust PC modifications

Standard PLA is great for many things, but special filaments can take your PC to the next level. You can use carbon fiber-infused materials to strengthen brackets, or flexible filaments like TPU to absorb vibrations near fans and mechanical drives.

Different materials also allow you to experiment with temperature, nozzle sizes, and print settings. For example, you might find that a specific filament is perfect for a hot part or that another helps reduce noise in a particular area.

Playing with different filament types teaches you how to balance performance, strength, and looks. Over time, you can start to associate specific materials with different parts of your PC, like high-load mounts or functional PC case panels. This helps you learn more and makes it easier to create more cool mods.

7 Develop essential design and CAD skills

Master software techniques for creative problem-solving in PC builds

Learning design and computer-aided design (CAD) software can boost your building skills. You can make simple changes to a bracket or create something totally new, and it’ll be so much easier if you know how to draw and measure your ideas accurately. This way, you can handle any hardware problems that don’t have a pre-made solution.

Using 3D modeling tools makes you think about how big, how close, and how well things fit together. You’ll learn how to create strong and easy-to-assemble parts with practice. This can even help you with your own projects, and you might even start sharing your creations with others. Getting feedback and new ideas can really help you improve your skills.

You might even start designing parts for friends or sharing your creations with the community as you get better. Seeing your ideas come to life for others can be truly rewarding. Over time, you’ll learn how to turn your thoughts into real things, and that’s one of the most extraordinary things about 3D printing and PC building.

6 Prototype exciting designs at home

Test and refine custom parts with quick turnaround times

Imagine having a magical tool that can turn your wildest creative ideas into physical objects in just a few hours. With a 3D printer, you can sketch your design, print it, and watch it come to life right before your eyes. This instant feedback speeds up your development process and helps you make the best decisions.

But here’s the cool part: you can keep changing and improving your design as you go. This hands-on experimentation with prototypes feels like playing with toys but can lead to some seriously innovative solutions. For example, you could try different cable routes or find a better way to mount radiators.

This practical experience also makes you a better 3D modeler. With each iteration and redesign, you learn more and more about how things work together. This practical learning experience builds confidence and helps you create better parts over time. So, if you’re up for a challenge, a 3D printer is your ticket to unlocking your creativity and making amazing things happen.

5 Replace broken parts with ease

Extend the life of components no longer in production

It can be a real pain when tiny plastic parts break on old cases or peripherals, and replacements are hard to find. However, a 3D printer can save the day. It lets you make your own stuff, like hinges, clips, or drive bay adapters that manufacturers have stopped making.

Printing your own spare parts is way faster than searching for used ones or waiting for them to ship. Once you have a good model, you can print it repeatedly, which is especially helpful if multiple parts have the same problem.

Many hobbyists keep a collection of replacement parts on hand to be ready for unexpected repairs. You can find or share these files online, which helps keep e-waste down, since there won’t be as many parts manufactured.

4 Build helpful desk and gaming accessories

Keep your workspace organized with unique printed solutions

Imagine having a 3D printer that goes beyond making simple PC upgrades. Think of your 3D printer as a personal design assistant that helps you create the perfect desk setup. Headphone stands, monitor risers, and controller mounts are just a few examples of how you can keep your workspace clutter-free and easily accessible. These custom accessories are made just for you, based on your desk layout and personal style.

You can also create your own storage solutions for all your PC components - think bins, racks, or holders that fit perfectly with your screws, cables, and other essentials. This way, everything is organized and safe, so you never have to worry about losing something important.

You can customize everything to fit your workspace perfectly. With user-friendly modeling software, you can ensure that each design is exactly the right size and shape for your desk. Plus, you can change the color, shape, and finish to match your PC and peripherals. This way, your workspace will look like a piece of art you’ve created yourself.

The best part is that you can easily change your designs as your needs change. You'll be able to add slots for new devices or make other changes that you think would be cool.

3 Explore new personalization and esthetics

Show off distinctive themes and custom decorative elements

PC enthusiasts love to show off their unique builds. A 3D printer lets you make cool fan grills, front-panel logos, or fancy accents that match your favorite colors or lighting.

Printing new panels or cool mods can give an old PC a new look. You can make anything you want, from simple accents to complex designs. Even a tiny detail can make your PC stand out.

You can try making more complex shapes or adding layers as you get better. This makes your PC even more unique and distinct from everyone else’s. You might even decide to print and build your own ergonomic mouse!

2 Design custom parts and adapters

Improve hardware mounting, airflow control, and overall stability

With a 3D printer, you can build your own parts exactly how you want them. Imagine making fan mounts, shrouds, and cable guides that fit perfectly into your case or motherboard. This is especially useful if you have a unique setup or want to make your PC run faster.

You can also print custom adapters to guide airflow, making your PC cooler and quieter. If you are concerned about GPU sag, you can make supports that match your GPU and case, keeping it straight and stable.

By making your own parts, you can save money and make your PC look and perform better than with pre-made components.

1 Enjoy cost savings and convenience

Avoid paying premiums and waiting for specialized parts

Specialized case components and modding accessories can be pricey or hard to find, especially if they’re no longer made. However, with a 3D printer, you can avoid high prices and supply issues. Just design what you want and print it right away. No more waiting for shipping or wondering if you’ll get the needed parts.

If you find a cool mod online or make one yourself, you can print it whenever you want without worrying about running out. Sure, the printer is an initial investment, but it’ll pay for itself in the long run. Printing your own stuff over and over saves you money, and you can always change your mind and make changes to the design as you go.

Final thoughts for dedicated PC enthusiasts to consider

A 3D printer gives you a magical toolbox that lets you build anything you can imagine. It’s both creative and practical, and it can help you fix broken parts and make your own creations for your PC. Once you start printing your own PC accessories and upgrades, you’ll be amazed at how much you can improve your hardware and design skills.

item.title: 6 essential NAS features your business shouldn’t ignore in 2025
item.itunes:title: 6 essential NAS features your business shouldn’t ignore in 2025
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There are quite a few ways your typical home NAS differs from its enterprise counterpart, most of which have to do with how the NAS holds, processes, and distributes data. Unlike home users, businesses face far more advanced and taxing needs — managing multiple users, applications, and environments. To handle all this complexity, it is crucial for businesses to leverage all the tools at their disposal to maximize the potential of their NAS while ensuring quick accessibility, data safety, and readiness for any data crisis.

Here are some NAS features that businesses shouldn’t ignore going forward in 2025 and should ask their IT administrators to set up immediately.

6 Cloud integration

Hybrid workflows are the way to go

Source: mockup.photos

Most NAS makers allow you to directly sync your files stored on the NAS with online services like Google Drive and Microsoft OneDrive. You can automate the backup of a portion of your files (such as only the important ones) or even the entire NAS using these tools. This ensures your files are seamlessly available and up-to-date across all platforms that your employees already have integrated into their workflow.

Having an off-site backup in the cloud also prepares you for unfortunate events like drive failures or disasters. Your files remain safe even if the NAS is damaged or faces a ransomware attack, ensuring minimal downtime and easy data recovery. Additionally, you can set up this hybrid system to keep frequently accessed files offline while others remain online, offering cost and time savings with adequate utilization of resources.

5 High-speed connectivity

Who doesn’t like faster file transfers?

Fast transfer speeds become essential rather than an optional upgrade when tens or even hundreds of people access the NAS simultaneously. This needs to be decided early in your journey — ideally before purchasing a new NAS. While many NAS makers are still cutting corners by offering only 1GbE ports (not even 2.5GbE), you should prioritize getting a model that supports 10GbE connectivity or at least has an upgrade option to enable faster throughput later on.

In case you’re not looking to upgrade your NAS in the near future, you can set up link aggregation on your existing device. While it won’t double the transfer speed for a single transfer stream, it increases overall bandwidth, speeding things up significantly for everyone on the network. Better load balancing also minimizes network bottlenecks by spreading out the load, ensuring smoother operations and improved productivity.

 Related
5 reasons you should use link aggregation on your NAS

Both home users and businesses can benefit from it

9

4 Automated backup scheduling

So obvious, yet often overlooked

If you’re still relying on manual backups, you’re exposing your NAS and the data in it to unnecessary risks — not to mention the gap between the last backup and a potential attack that could result in complete loss of data for that period. Depending on how frequently your data changes and its importance, you can configure the NAS to perform automatic backups hourly, daily, or weekly. Hourly backups are ideal for keeping critical files up to date everywhere, while daily or weekly backups are sufficient for less vital data.

A good thing is that you can granularly configure this to create custom schedules for different data types on the same NAS. As mentioned earlier, you should set up these automated backups to save copies of your data to an off-site location, saving you from events where your main NAS is compromised or damaged. In addition to that, incremental backups are particularly useful as they don’t overload the network during the backup process, keeping things efficient.

3 Advanced file versioning

A well-thought-out retention policy goes a long way

Source: Unsplash

Your IT manager can implement a company-wide file versioning and retention policy applicable to all data that you generate or process. For instance, saving up to 20 historical file versions updated in real-time enables you to track changes and revert to a previous state if a file gets corrupted or unwanted edits are made. Additionally, holding a client’s files for a defined period after contract termination may be a regulatory requirement, which this feature helps fulfill.

While keeping historical records of your data is important, it’s even more crucial to minimize the recovery time following any disruption. You should ideally aim for a net-zero recovery time objective (RTO), but any reduction in downtime is welcome. In the event of data loss, you can quickly restore important files to get back on track while other files are recovered in the background. Robust file retention paired with easy and quick recovery ensures your office NAS remains useful in every situation.

 Related
What is data restoration, and how does it work?

Not all deleted data is truly gone, and data restoration can pull it back off your hard drive or SSD in some situations

2 AI-powered search tools

Put AI to some good use in 2025

Gone are the days when you needed to remember exact names and use specific keywords to find your files. Modern NAS systems leverage AI capabilities, including natural language processing (NLP), that will allow users to search for files using simple queries in everyday language. This not only enhances the user experience but also boosts productivity with predictive capabilities that anticipate the files you might need at a particular time.

Many NAS brands are already implementing such features and are also using AI to automatically tag and classify files, just like Google Photos groups images by face all by itself. This allows your NAS to bundle your frequently accessed files into dedicated folders or display them prominently in the file manager. These smart features simplify routine tasks, making your team more efficient. However, running these AI features may require newer hardware unless NAS makers decide to bring them to their existing models.

1 Multi-layered ransomware prevention

You have to be prepared for the worst

Targeted ransomware attacks on servers will only get more sophisticated in the future. The best defense against them is a multi-layered approach to minimize the risk of an attack in the first place and reduce damage if an attack occurs. A good measure is to limit your NAS’s external exposure. If that’s not feasible, consider setting up multiple NAS units, with some disconnected from the internet to house critical business data securely.

Attackers often exploit unpatched vulnerabilities. To prevent that, regularly update your NAS firmware and ensure that employee devices accessing the NAS have updated client software. Installing anti-ransomware tools adds another layer of protection. Finally, you should enforce granular user permissions with user groups to restrict access to sensitive data only to authorized employees, ensuring that even if one profile is compromised accidentally, the damage is limited.

2025 is the year to prioritize data safety

Businesses walk a tightrope with their data. Their NAS units are almost always under heavy load, and yet they must stay reliably fast without disruptions. On top of that, businesses constantly need to keep an eye out for possible threats that could compromise data integrity. That’s a lot to expect from your office storage setup, yet none of this can be avoided. But with basic housekeeping and by implementing these advanced measures, you can keep your NAS running smoothly and your data safe.

Here are some NAS features that businesses shouldn’t ignore going forward in 2025 and should ask their IT administrators to set up immediately.

6 Cloud integration

Hybrid workflows are the way to go

Source: mockup.photos

Most NAS makers allow you to directly sync your files stored on the NAS with online services like Google Drive and Microsoft OneDrive. You can automate the backup of a portion of your files (such as only the important ones) or even the entire NAS using these tools. This ensures your files are seamlessly available and up-to-date across all platforms that your employees already have integrated into their workflow.

Having an off-site backup in the cloud also prepares you for unfortunate events like drive failures or disasters. Your files remain safe even if the NAS is damaged or faces a ransomware attack, ensuring minimal downtime and easy data recovery. Additionally, you can set up this hybrid system to keep frequently accessed files offline while others remain online, offering cost and time savings with adequate utilization of resources.

5 High-speed connectivity

Who doesn’t like faster file transfers?

Fast transfer speeds become essential rather than an optional upgrade when tens or even hundreds of people access the NAS simultaneously. This needs to be decided early in your journey — ideally before purchasing a new NAS. While many NAS makers are still cutting corners by offering only 1GbE ports (not even 2.5GbE), you should prioritize getting a model that supports 10GbE connectivity or at least has an upgrade option to enable faster throughput later on.

In case you’re not looking to upgrade your NAS in the near future, you can set up link aggregation on your existing device. While it won’t double the transfer speed for a single transfer stream, it increases overall bandwidth, speeding things up significantly for everyone on the network. Better load balancing also minimizes network bottlenecks by spreading out the load, ensuring smoother operations and improved productivity.

 Related
5 reasons you should use link aggregation on your NAS

Both home users and businesses can benefit from it

9

4 Automated backup scheduling

So obvious, yet often overlooked

If you’re still relying on manual backups, you’re exposing your NAS and the data in it to unnecessary risks — not to mention the gap between the last backup and a potential attack that could result in complete loss of data for that period. Depending on how frequently your data changes and its importance, you can configure the NAS to perform automatic backups hourly, daily, or weekly. Hourly backups are ideal for keeping critical files up to date everywhere, while daily or weekly backups are sufficient for less vital data.

A good thing is that you can granularly configure this to create custom schedules for different data types on the same NAS. As mentioned earlier, you should set up these automated backups to save copies of your data to an off-site location, saving you from events where your main NAS is compromised or damaged. In addition to that, incremental backups are particularly useful as they don’t overload the network during the backup process, keeping things efficient.

3 Advanced file versioning

A well-thought-out retention policy goes a long way

Source: Unsplash

Your IT manager can implement a company-wide file versioning and retention policy applicable to all data that you generate or process. For instance, saving up to 20 historical file versions updated in real-time enables you to track changes and revert to a previous state if a file gets corrupted or unwanted edits are made. Additionally, holding a client’s files for a defined period after contract termination may be a regulatory requirement, which this feature helps fulfill.

While keeping historical records of your data is important, it’s even more crucial to minimize the recovery time following any disruption. You should ideally aim for a net-zero recovery time objective (RTO), but any reduction in downtime is welcome. In the event of data loss, you can quickly restore important files to get back on track while other files are recovered in the background. Robust file retention paired with easy and quick recovery ensures your office NAS remains useful in every situation.

 Related
What is data restoration, and how does it work?

Not all deleted data is truly gone, and data restoration can pull it back off your hard drive or SSD in some situations

2 AI-powered search tools

Put AI to some good use in 2025

Gone are the days when you needed to remember exact names and use specific keywords to find your files. Modern NAS systems leverage AI capabilities, including natural language processing (NLP), that will allow users to search for files using simple queries in everyday language. This not only enhances the user experience but also boosts productivity with predictive capabilities that anticipate the files you might need at a particular time.

Many NAS brands are already implementing such features and are also using AI to automatically tag and classify files, just like Google Photos groups images by face all by itself. This allows your NAS to bundle your frequently accessed files into dedicated folders or display them prominently in the file manager. These smart features simplify routine tasks, making your team more efficient. However, running these AI features may require newer hardware unless NAS makers decide to bring them to their existing models.

1 Multi-layered ransomware prevention

You have to be prepared for the worst

Targeted ransomware attacks on servers will only get more sophisticated in the future. The best defense against them is a multi-layered approach to minimize the risk of an attack in the first place and reduce damage if an attack occurs. A good measure is to limit your NAS’s external exposure. If that’s not feasible, consider setting up multiple NAS units, with some disconnected from the internet to house critical business data securely.

Attackers often exploit unpatched vulnerabilities. To prevent that, regularly update your NAS firmware and ensure that employee devices accessing the NAS have updated client software. Installing anti-ransomware tools adds another layer of protection. Finally, you should enforce granular user permissions with user groups to restrict access to sensitive data only to authorized employees, ensuring that even if one profile is compromised accidentally, the damage is limited.

2025 is the year to prioritize data safety

Businesses walk a tightrope with their data. Their NAS units are almost always under heavy load, and yet they must stay reliably fast without disruptions. On top of that, businesses constantly need to keep an eye out for possible threats that could compromise data integrity. That’s a lot to expect from your office storage setup, yet none of this can be avoided. But with basic housekeeping and by implementing these advanced measures, you can keep your NAS running smoothly and your data safe.

Source: mockup.photos

Both home users and businesses can benefit from it

Source: Unsplash

item.title: 10 retro operating systems you can try out for a shot of nostalgia
item.itunes:title: 10 retro operating systems you can try out for a shot of nostalgia
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Retro gaming is having a huge revival right now, with hardware emulating classic consoles so you can use original cartridges, to emulators to create retro PCs, and Atari releasing a modern version of the 7800 from the mid-80s for you to use any old cartridges you still have in the attic. It's a fantastic time to be a classic gaming fan, but what about the operating systems that we used to use?

Before Windows and macOS came and took over the computing landscape, computers ran various versions of DOS or competing versions of GUIs with differing architectures under the hood. Some of these classic OSes have been either faithfully maintained and modernized somewhat or recreated with the Linux kernel underneath, and some of this list is truly one-of-a-kind.

If you feel nostalgic for how you used to write documents in the 1980s or want to remind yourself how much better modern operating systems are, take a trip down this list. You might find something you want to use daily.

 Related
Is it time for an operating system built from scratch?

Back to the drawing board?

30

10 ProDOS

Did you know you could still use the OS from the Apple IIc?

My personal computing journey started on an Apple IIc, not that long after it was released in the early 80s. Before the days of Intel x86 chips being in every computer, the Apple II range was among the powerhouses of personal computing, with productivity apps and games all rendered in glorious 8-bit. While Apple stopped officially releasing ProDOS in 1993, it got resurrected by an Apple ][ developer called John Brooks in 2016, the 30th anniversary of the Apple IIgs.

While you might need Apple II hardware to run ProDOS in the form available from ProDOS8, you can download emulators like AppleWin or Epple-II and a disk image and enjoy the splendor of 8-bit, green monochrome or high-resolution color graphics in 80-column text. The monochrome green calls to me more, but maybe that's because I was always jealous of the color monitor owners.

ProDOS
See at Official Site

9 SerenityOS

Want to play with a Unix-like OS from the 90s?

While many retro-feeling operating systems are reskins of *nix kernels, SerenityOS is different. It's not a skin but was built from the ground up to be totally different. It's the brainchild of Andreas Kling, who started dabbling in low-level operating system stuff after getting out of rehab in 2018. He set a goal to build his "own dream system for daily use," and SerenityOS was born. It's a strange mashup of retro Windows UI simplicity but with a deep open-source philosophy under the hood, with a bunch of common applications for web browsing and code editing.

If you don't want to jump into a full retro OS, you could try the Ladybird web browser, another project by Andreas that's written to be cross-platform. He used his experience working at Apple to write full new browser engines for the web engine, graphics, JS replacement, and more, but be warned that it's not ready for daily use. The edges are many and they are rough.

SerenityOS
See at Official Site

8 Chicago95

Now you can (sort of) use Windows 95 on modern hardware without all the malware

Want to experience what Windows 95 felt like without having to install your internet from a demo disc of Netscape Navigator? Well, you're in luck because Chicago95 is a deeply customized Linux theme that gives you all the 32-bit goodness of Win95 while saving you from the dial-up tones of a modem connection that always seemed louder at night.

It's not a simple reskin either, with everything from the Start menu and taskbar faithfully recreated for you to use. There's even an automated installer that mimics the installers of Windows from that era. It is truly a work of mad passion by its developer, and it is worth checking out just for that.

Chicago95
See at Official Site

7 Not so Common Desktop Environment (NsCDE)

Get a feel for how the Unix CDE used to be

One of the main aspects of Linux that attracts users is how much choice it offers. It's not just in applications, but the desktop environment can be changed, tweaked, and otherwise adjusted to fit how you want to use your computer. You can make it feel more modern, or more like macOS, or if you really desire, like a classic throwback to Unix computing.

Not so Common Desktop Environment is a desktop environment to run over a Linux installation that looks and feels like the Common Desktop Environment (CDE) that ran on HP Unix systems. Enjoy a desktop full of 90s-era pastel shades while enjoying the modern conveniences of a recent Linux distro.

Not so Common Desktop Environment
See at Official Site

6 Snowdrop

This 16-bit OS built for the IBM PC architecture is fully homebrew and written in x86 assembly

While most operating systems are built by teams of developers, Snowdrop OS is the creation of one man, Sebastian Mihai. It's completely coded in x86 assembly for bare metal access, runs on a potato (figuratively speaking), and is fairly functional if you don't mind tweaking things yourself.

The full source code is available, and you can boot it from a floppy disk image (if you have vintage hardware around), or as a CD-ROM image that can be installed onto a hard disk. Again, everything was hand-coded from scratch, from the boot loader to the kernel, the GUI, and the programs that run on it. It's a fascinating glimpse into how things were at one time and how operating systems don't have to be built in the same way, even if they use similar concepts and abstractions.

Snowdrop
See at Official Site

5 Haiku

Inspired by BeOS and made for speed and ease of use

Ahh, the 90s. Neon, mullets, cartoons designed to sell toys to kids—what a time! It was a heady environment for multiple competing personal computing OSes. Be Inc. used to make BeBox computers and BeOS, the operating system that ran on them, when PowerPC CPUs were the rage. BeOS outlived the hardware but didn't outlive a sale to Palm, Inc. in 2001. One developer decided to keep BeOS alive by creating an open-source, backward-compatible replacement, and what became Haiku OS was born.

Haiku
See at Official Site

4 Amiga Forever

Relive the Amiga with this official emulator that keeps getting new features

Emulation often gets a bad reputation because there's a thorny situation where retro apps and games are no longer developed and abandoned by their rights holders but still available to download and run in emulators. But Amiga Forever takes a different stance, as officially licensed emulation and preservation for the Amiga and its apps, games, and more. As such, it's the only operating system on this list that costs money, but it does come with every version of the official Amiga ROM and OS files, and it's even got an Android app, if you prefer to do your emulation on the go.

Amiga Forever
$40 at Official Site

3 ARAnyM

Run the operating system from the Atari on pretty much any machine you own

ARAnyM is a shortened acronym for Atari Running on Any Machine, to emulate the operating systems used on various Atari machines on modern hardware. That means you can easily install it (and there are many pre-compiled versions to do so) and enjoy the feel and look of the classic Atari home computers of the company's heyday.

ARAnyM
See at Official Site

2 KolibriOS

Run an entire operating system on only a few MB of disk and memory space

While most modern operating systems are sprawling beasts that eat up disk space, KolibriOS is tiny. It can fit on one 1.44MB floppy disk, which is a staggering achievement on its own to have modern connectivity and file systems. It boots in seconds, has hundreds of inbuilt apps, a GUI, and support for common USB devices like mice, hubs, keyboards, and flash drives. Oh, and it only needs 8MB of RAM, so it can run on almost anything with a CPU inside.

KolibriOS
See at Official Site

1 ReactOS

Run Windows applications and drivers in an open-source operating system written from scratch

Windows might be the most used operating system for home users, but what if you don't want to use Microsoft's OS but do want to use the apps written to run on it? Before SteamOS and the Proton compatibility layer added that functionality to Linux, ReactOS was the only answer to that question. It's an open-source clone of Windows NT that is fully compatible with any app, driver, and service that also runs on Windows NT.

 Related
ReactOS hands-on: An open-source operating system for those who miss Windows XP

It's not perfect, but it marks a big step forward for the bloat-free Windows experience

As such, it's probably the easiest retro-feeling OS to install and use because it feels familiar while offering modern conveniences and stability. It can also be run from a live USB, so you don't even have to install it on your internal hard drive if you prefer to test-drive things before proceeding.

ReactOS
See at ReactOS Project

These are only a few of the classic operating systems you can still use today

Whether you want to experience how BASIC some of the early operating systems used on computers of yesteryear or prefer the modern conveniences of today's OSes with a retro feel, there are plenty of people maintaining classic operating systems that still run on current hardware. However, the best way, in my experience, is by running them in a virtual machine, so you abstract the hardware requirements out. It's also often a more pleasant experience to emulate classic games for the same reason, and many retro OSes were before the UX (user experience) field was quantified.

 Related
I use DOSBox to play the games that first got me into gaming, and these 10 are my favorites

Take a trip down memory lane with me with DOSBox and some classic titles from the late 80s and early 90s

6

Retro gaming is having a huge revival right now, with hardware emulating classic consoles so you can use original cartridges, to emulators to create retro PCs, and Atari releasing a modern version of the 7800 from the mid-80s for you to use any old cartridges you still have in the attic. It's a fantastic time to be a classic gaming fan, but what about the operating systems that we used to use?

Before Windows and macOS came and took over the computing landscape, computers ran various versions of DOS or competing versions of GUIs with differing architectures under the hood. Some of these classic OSes have been either faithfully maintained and modernized somewhat or recreated with the Linux kernel underneath, and some of this list is truly one-of-a-kind.

If you feel nostalgic for how you used to write documents in the 1980s or want to remind yourself how much better modern operating systems are, take a trip down this list. You might find something you want to use daily.

 Related
Is it time for an operating system built from scratch?

Back to the drawing board?

30

10 ProDOS

Did you know you could still use the OS from the Apple IIc?

My personal computing journey started on an Apple IIc, not that long after it was released in the early 80s. Before the days of Intel x86 chips being in every computer, the Apple II range was among the powerhouses of personal computing, with productivity apps and games all rendered in glorious 8-bit. While Apple stopped officially releasing ProDOS in 1993, it got resurrected by an Apple ][ developer called John Brooks in 2016, the 30th anniversary of the Apple IIgs.

While you might need Apple II hardware to run ProDOS in the form available from ProDOS8, you can download emulators like AppleWin or Epple-II and a disk image and enjoy the splendor of 8-bit, green monochrome or high-resolution color graphics in 80-column text. The monochrome green calls to me more, but maybe that's because I was always jealous of the color monitor owners.

ProDOS
See at Official Site

9 SerenityOS

Want to play with a Unix-like OS from the 90s?

While many retro-feeling operating systems are reskins of *nix kernels, SerenityOS is different. It's not a skin but was built from the ground up to be totally different. It's the brainchild of Andreas Kling, who started dabbling in low-level operating system stuff after getting out of rehab in 2018. He set a goal to build his "own dream system for daily use," and SerenityOS was born. It's a strange mashup of retro Windows UI simplicity but with a deep open-source philosophy under the hood, with a bunch of common applications for web browsing and code editing.

If you don't want to jump into a full retro OS, you could try the Ladybird web browser, another project by Andreas that's written to be cross-platform. He used his experience working at Apple to write full new browser engines for the web engine, graphics, JS replacement, and more, but be warned that it's not ready for daily use. The edges are many and they are rough.

SerenityOS
See at Official Site

8 Chicago95

Now you can (sort of) use Windows 95 on modern hardware without all the malware

Want to experience what Windows 95 felt like without having to install your internet from a demo disc of Netscape Navigator? Well, you're in luck because Chicago95 is a deeply customized Linux theme that gives you all the 32-bit goodness of Win95 while saving you from the dial-up tones of a modem connection that always seemed louder at night.

It's not a simple reskin either, with everything from the Start menu and taskbar faithfully recreated for you to use. There's even an automated installer that mimics the installers of Windows from that era. It is truly a work of mad passion by its developer, and it is worth checking out just for that.

Chicago95
See at Official Site

7 Not so Common Desktop Environment (NsCDE)

Get a feel for how the Unix CDE used to be

One of the main aspects of Linux that attracts users is how much choice it offers. It's not just in applications, but the desktop environment can be changed, tweaked, and otherwise adjusted to fit how you want to use your computer. You can make it feel more modern, or more like macOS, or if you really desire, like a classic throwback to Unix computing.

Not so Common Desktop Environment is a desktop environment to run over a Linux installation that looks and feels like the Common Desktop Environment (CDE) that ran on HP Unix systems. Enjoy a desktop full of 90s-era pastel shades while enjoying the modern conveniences of a recent Linux distro.

Not so Common Desktop Environment
See at Official Site

6 Snowdrop

This 16-bit OS built for the IBM PC architecture is fully homebrew and written in x86 assembly

While most operating systems are built by teams of developers, Snowdrop OS is the creation of one man, Sebastian Mihai. It's completely coded in x86 assembly for bare metal access, runs on a potato (figuratively speaking), and is fairly functional if you don't mind tweaking things yourself.

The full source code is available, and you can boot it from a floppy disk image (if you have vintage hardware around), or as a CD-ROM image that can be installed onto a hard disk. Again, everything was hand-coded from scratch, from the boot loader to the kernel, the GUI, and the programs that run on it. It's a fascinating glimpse into how things were at one time and how operating systems don't have to be built in the same way, even if they use similar concepts and abstractions.

Snowdrop
See at Official Site

5 Haiku

Inspired by BeOS and made for speed and ease of use

Ahh, the 90s. Neon, mullets, cartoons designed to sell toys to kids—what a time! It was a heady environment for multiple competing personal computing OSes. Be Inc. used to make BeBox computers and BeOS, the operating system that ran on them, when PowerPC CPUs were the rage. BeOS outlived the hardware but didn't outlive a sale to Palm, Inc. in 2001. One developer decided to keep BeOS alive by creating an open-source, backward-compatible replacement, and what became Haiku OS was born.

Haiku
See at Official Site

4 Amiga Forever

Relive the Amiga with this official emulator that keeps getting new features

Emulation often gets a bad reputation because there's a thorny situation where retro apps and games are no longer developed and abandoned by their rights holders but still available to download and run in emulators. But Amiga Forever takes a different stance, as officially licensed emulation and preservation for the Amiga and its apps, games, and more. As such, it's the only operating system on this list that costs money, but it does come with every version of the official Amiga ROM and OS files, and it's even got an Android app, if you prefer to do your emulation on the go.

Amiga Forever
$40 at Official Site

3 ARAnyM

Run the operating system from the Atari on pretty much any machine you own

ARAnyM is a shortened acronym for Atari Running on Any Machine, to emulate the operating systems used on various Atari machines on modern hardware. That means you can easily install it (and there are many pre-compiled versions to do so) and enjoy the feel and look of the classic Atari home computers of the company's heyday.

ARAnyM
See at Official Site

2 KolibriOS

Run an entire operating system on only a few MB of disk and memory space

While most modern operating systems are sprawling beasts that eat up disk space, KolibriOS is tiny. It can fit on one 1.44MB floppy disk, which is a staggering achievement on its own to have modern connectivity and file systems. It boots in seconds, has hundreds of inbuilt apps, a GUI, and support for common USB devices like mice, hubs, keyboards, and flash drives. Oh, and it only needs 8MB of RAM, so it can run on almost anything with a CPU inside.

KolibriOS
See at Official Site

1 ReactOS

Run Windows applications and drivers in an open-source operating system written from scratch

Windows might be the most used operating system for home users, but what if you don't want to use Microsoft's OS but do want to use the apps written to run on it? Before SteamOS and the Proton compatibility layer added that functionality to Linux, ReactOS was the only answer to that question. It's an open-source clone of Windows NT that is fully compatible with any app, driver, and service that also runs on Windows NT.

 Related
ReactOS hands-on: An open-source operating system for those who miss Windows XP

It's not perfect, but it marks a big step forward for the bloat-free Windows experience

As such, it's probably the easiest retro-feeling OS to install and use because it feels familiar while offering modern conveniences and stability. It can also be run from a live USB, so you don't even have to install it on your internal hard drive if you prefer to test-drive things before proceeding.

ReactOS
See at ReactOS Project

These are only a few of the classic operating systems you can still use today

Whether you want to experience how BASIC some of the early operating systems used on computers of yesteryear or prefer the modern conveniences of today's OSes with a retro feel, there are plenty of people maintaining classic operating systems that still run on current hardware. However, the best way, in my experience, is by running them in a virtual machine, so you abstract the hardware requirements out. It's also often a more pleasant experience to emulate classic games for the same reason, and many retro OSes were before the UX (user experience) field was quantified.

 Related
I use DOSBox to play the games that first got me into gaming, and these 10 are my favorites

Take a trip down memory lane with me with DOSBox and some classic titles from the late 80s and early 90s

6

item.title: 5 reasons eGPU setups might be making a comeback
item.itunes:title: 5 reasons eGPU setups might be making a comeback
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When first introduced to the market, external graphics cards or eGPUs were hailed as the savior of gaming on the go, able to increase the graphical power of the best laptops by offloading the processing to the eGPU. While this was a fantastic idea in theory, the practical applications and slower connectivity standards of the time conspired to make them lackluster at best, and the market went stagnant. Most of the eGPU enclosures available now were released years ago, and the manufacturers haven't really been in a rush to release updated versions.

But technology goes on, and sometimes it's a situation of the right idea but at the wrong time. Since the first eGPUs, connectivity standards have improved, both in terms of bandwidth and the protocols that can transmit over them, and USB4 brought Thunderbolt-like capabilities to many more devices. Add in the explosive popularity of PC gaming handhelds, and the conditions are right for a resurgence of eGPUs.

Related
3 ways to turn an old GPU into your own eGPU

Give your laptop or PC gaming handheld some extra graphical oomph.

2

5 The rise of PC gaming handhelds

Handhelds are crying out for more power when in docked mode

While other technologies make eGPUs viable in theory, one section of the gaming market makes them viable commercially. One big reason eGPUs never quite took off is that they needed to be connected to laptops, and the vast majority of laptop users don't care about gaming. They have laptops for work, they do work with them, and that's the end of things. But the popularity of gaming handhelds means there is a captive audience for eGPUs. The Nintendo Switch famously gives you more performance when used in docked mode, and PC gaming handhelds have a variety of existing eGPU solutions to give a similar boost to graphical performance.

The ROG Ally used the ROG Xg Mobile interface to connect to the ROG Xg Mobile eGPU dock, which offered several options for GPU performance and added features like Ethernet, more USB ports, and HDMI and DP for external monitor use. The dock might have been overpriced, and Asus switched the port to USB4 instead with the ROG Ally X, but it showed that eGPU docks were viable for mobile gamers.

The Xg Mobile dock was one of the first things many of my friends got when they ordered their ROG Ally, and I can't remember the last time anyone I know bought an eGPU for their laptop. Then there's the crop of mini PCs and handhelds that use the newer OCuLinK standard, which promises multiple times the bandwidth of Thunderbolt 4.

The other common thread here is that every PC gaming handheld, from the Steam Deck to the Legion Go, has a desktop mode, so they can be used as computer replacements with the right dock. That dock might as well increase the graphical power while it's increasing the number of viable ports for peripherals and monitors, giving eGPUs a new audience.

 Related
I tried an OCuLink external GPU — is it actually better than USB4?

Could OCuLink rahert USB4 and Thunderbolt obsolete?

4

4 Thunderbolt 5 is here

Okay, it's kind of here, but it's available and brings lots of bandwidth

Early eGPU solutions used Thunderbolt, but the early versions of Thunderbolt were limited in bandwidth, limiting the effective power of the graphics cards installed in the enclosures. In 2017, we tested an Nvidia GTX 1080 inside a Thunderbolt 3 enclosure, and the performance was anywhere between 12% and 20% lower when installed in the enclosure. It's not terrible, but it's also not impressive, considering you had to spend $300 for the enclosure and whatever the cost of your graphics card was.

But Thunderbolt 5 is now here, and among the other things it promises, you can get up to 120Gbps of dynamic bandwidth in one direction. Perfect for high-performance storage options, sure. But it's even more exciting for gaming use because it means more bandwidth for eGPU use. Not as much as plugging that GPU into a PCIe slot in a desktop PC, for sure, but enough for mid-range GPUs to shine. Flagships like the GeForce RTX 5090 and AMD RX 7900 XTX are going to need more bandwidth than even TB5 can supply, but maybe future versions will close that gap.

 Related
3 reasons Thunderbolt 5 is making external GPUs more viable than ever

Thunderbolt is back and it's better than ever, but could we see a resurgence in eGPUs?

3 Smaller and more powerful GPUs just arrived

Nvidia's RTX 5000 FE models are small form factor ready

Over the last few generations of discrete graphics cards, the average size has trended up, leading to cards that almost hit a four-slot thickness. Most eGPU enclosures support a GPU that's 2-slots width, at the most. I'm sure you can see what the issue here is, and it's a big one because it limits the power of the GPU that you can even choose, before you get to the issue of performance drops from using an eGPU enclosure. The RTX 4090 FE is a three-slot solution (barely), but even that's too much unless you're making your own eGPU that doesn't have an enclosure.

However, Nvidia's GeForce RTX 50 series promises smaller, more powerful GPUs. At least, if you can get one of the Founders Edition models, because the company has said they're two-slot and small form-factor ready. That means they'll happily pop into the eGPU enclosures on the market, although you might need to upgrade the PSU that they come with, depending on which RTX 50 series card you're looking at.

 Related
Don't forget these 5 things before building a small form factor PC

An SFF PC can be an exciting project, but keep these things in mind before you start building one

2 The rise of soldered-on components

Laptops solder on components to save space but then you run out of performance

Whether it's to make slimline laptops, decrease RAM latency by putting the memory chips on the same PCB (or in some cases, on the CPU), or to save space in PC gaming handhelds or mini PCs, soldered components are everywhere. That means CPUs, memory, storage, and GPUs on the same mainboard, with little or no option to upgrade when new technologies come along or if you run out of storage space.

Which is fine, really, as you're trading future upgrades for actually being able to carry your laptop around all day. But when you're back at home, the original promise of eGPUs can come into play, giving you more GPU grunt, optional speedy storage space, and more ports that wouldn't fit on the thin edge of your laptop. It won't let you plug in more RAM, but even the base model MacBooks come with 16GB of memory now, so you should be okay for some time on that front.

 Related
4 reasons why repairability should be the next big thing for laptops

Easy-to-repair laptops need to become the norm

2

1 They weren't fast enough before

Bandwidth issues from earlier connectivity options made them subpar

When the Nvidia GeForce RTX 50 series hits shelves later this month, we'll have the first PCIe 5.0 graphics cards (finally!). But that means an increased amount of bandwidth required for peak performance, and that's just not possible with any of the current cable-connected connectivity standards. Thunderbolt 5, OCuLink, and USB4 aren't up to the task of comparing them to a physical PCIe slot on a motherboard, and neither were any of their predecessors.

The current crop of GPUs use PCIe 4.0 x16, which gives a theoretical maximum of 31.5GB/s of bandwidth, roughly eight times more bandwidth than Thunderbolt 4 can supply. Thunderbolt 5 closes that gap somewhat with three times the bandwidth of Thunderbolt 4 when the dynamic mode is enabled, but it's still not quite at PCIe 4.0 x16 levels.

OCuLink gets closer, with the first iteration bringing a theoretical 3.9GB/s and then a revision to 16GB/s, and OCuLink 2 gets all the way to 32GB/s of bandwidth in every direction when implemented as a four-lane implementation. That's just as much as an internal PCIe 4.0 x16 slot, making even flagship GPUs viable. The only question is how much performance is lost with overhead as the conversion to over-cable PCIe happens. That's a question that doesn't currently have an answer.

eGPUs are ready for a resurgence

Technological advances in connectivity and improvements to the protocols used in DisplayPort over cables make external GPUs more relevant than they ever have been. Plus, Thunderbolt 5 increases charging capacity to 240W over the same cable, making almost any laptop able to charge while docked. Add the runaway success of PC gaming handhelds and mini PCs, and you've got the right conditions for eGPUs to thrive.

OCuLink gets closer, with the first iteration bringing a theoretical 3.9GB/s and then a revision to 16GB/s, and OCuLink 2 gets all the way to 32GB/s of bandwidth in every direction when implemented as a four-lane implementation. That's just as much as an internal PCIe 4.0 x16 slot, making even flagship GPUs viable. The only question is how much performance is lost with overhead as the conversion to over-cable PCIe happens. That's a question that doesn't currently have an answer.

eGPUs are ready for a resurgence

Technological advances in connectivity and improvements to the protocols used in DisplayPort over cables make external GPUs more relevant than they ever have been. Plus, Thunderbolt 5 increases charging capacity to 240W over the same cable, making almost any laptop able to charge while docked. Add the runaway success of PC gaming handhelds and mini PCs, and you've got the right conditions for eGPUs to thrive.

item.title: 14 reasons why 2007 was the greatest year in video games
item.itunes:title: 14 reasons why 2007 was the greatest year in video games
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Crowning a single year as the GOAT for anything whatsoever is a controversial exercise; you're bound to anger someone or another. Doing it for video games is especially hard, because there have simply been too many great years that either propelled the industry forward or delivered a lifetime's worth of gaming memories.

Years like 1998, 2004, 2010, and 2013 gave us some of the most influential titles and consoles of all time, while 2017 and 2018 became known as the new golden age of gaming. However, if I had to choose one year (with a gun to my head) that not only gave us an unbelievably high number of stellar releases but also left an indelible mark on the industry (and gamers), I would pick 2007. And I have 14 reasons that will make my case.

 Related
10 best underrated games to play in 2025 instead of broken AAA titles

Disappointed with broken AAA games? Start your new year with these underrated indie gems

4

14 Metroid Prime 3: Corruption (Nintendo Wii)

Prime example of innovation done right

It says something about what's to come when you start the list with a game as remarkable as Metroid Prime 3: Corruption. The third entry in the Metroid series became the first true showcase of the Nintendo Wii's new motion controls, allowing players to shoot enemies with unprecedented accuracy, unlock doors, and shatter enemy shields in a satisfying fashion.

Retro Studios also fully used the nascent HD-era technologies to pack the game with visually stunning levels that stopped players in their tracks. What's more, you could control Samus' ship for the first time in the series to navigate to the various planets in the game or call in an artillery assist when stuck in a tight spot. Nintendo even ended up implementing the much-loved motion controls in the first two installments of the series.

Sadly, if you want to acquire the game today, you have to rely on the used market (Amazon, Walmart, eBay), since Nintendo no longer sells new copies.

Metroid Prime 3: Corruption

Metroid Prime 3: Corruption was the third installment in Retro Studios' trilogy, but it was probably the most influential one. It made full use of the Wii's motion controls, featured some of the most visually stunning environments in the series, and allowed players new ways to navigate between planets and enjoy the action. 

$10 at Amazon

13 Uncharted: Drake's Fortune (PS 3)

Naughty Dog in uncharted territory

PlayStation has given us landmark franchises that defined multiple console generations, and Uncharted is unequivocally one of the best. It all started in 2007 with Uncharted: Drake's Fortune as Naughty Dog stepped in a new direction, giving birth to the outspoken and reckless treasure hunter, Nathan Drake. The studio took players on the ride of a lifetime as Nate traveled the globe in search of riches and adventure, propelled by a deep down desire to prove himself worthy of his legacy.

The first game in the series, which has now become way bigger than the studio ever imagined, wasn't as well-rounded as the later titles in the series, but it's where it all started. The cinematic gameplay, chaotic combat, satisfying platforming, and a rich roster of lovable characters — these are the elements that went on to define the series. I discovered the franchise only with Uncharted 4: A Thief's End, but it didn't take me long to go back and experience the marvelous early days of Nathan Drake.

4 Images
4 Images
Uncharted: Drake's Fortune

A drastic departure from Naughty Dog's previous outings, Uncharted: Drake's Fortune kicked off a seminal franchise that would become one of the greatest in the console's history. The game sowed the seeds of treasure hunting, fast-paced action, cinematic gameplay, and an engrossing narrative that would define the entire series.

$15 at Playstation Store

12 Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare (PC, PS3, Xbox 360)

Back to the present

Call of Duty was already a four-year-old franchise by the time Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare came out in 2007. However, Infinity Ward wasn't content with revisiting the World War 2 era once again, opting instead to bring the series to a contemporary setting. Taking players across the globe, the game armed players with advanced technology and superior weapons in a fight against a rogue army, delivering a cinematic campaign rarely attempted before in the series.

While the single-player story gave rise to some of the most iconic missions in the series, the multiplayer component completely changed how gamers played online. Customizing loadouts with perks and attachments, chasing killstreaks and rank progression, and getting hooked to a detailed XP system became the new hallmarks of the modern FPS multiplayer experience. Even 18 years later, Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare's multiplayer servers are alive and kicking, proving the game's lasting appeal and impact.

Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare (2007)

Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare was a shot in the arm for military shooters that were filled with WW2-era campaigns in 2007. It not only gave players, well, modern warfare to get engrossed in, but also revitalized multiplayer gaming, inspiring many other games to come.

$20 at Steam $28 at Amazon

11 Crysis (PC)

Yes, it can finally run Crysis

"But can it run Crysis" became a meme, but the origins of the catchphrase highlighted the sheer technological superiority of Crysis, Crytek's first installment in the trilogy. The FPS offered players a power fantasy filled with an advanced nanosuit with superpowers, open-ended gameplay with futuristic weapons and vehicles, and a world that looked more realistic than anything that had come before.

Crytek's engine was so ahead of its time that barely anyone was able to play the game at maximum settings (no pun intended), but despite the generic story and setting, the game managed to set itself apart. The two sequels pushed things forward and stayed true to the series' defining traits — groundbreaking visuals and futuristic gameplay — but the first game can still hold its own. A remastered version was also released in 2021 for a generation of players and hardware.

6 Images
6 Images
Crysis

Crysis was responsible for pushing PC graphics forward in 2007, bringing rigs to their knees, thanks to its state-of-the-art graphics. The story might have been cliche, but the high-tech nanosuit, alien tech, and varied missions made the FPS a household name.

$20 at GOG $30 at Steam

10 God of War II (PS2)

It's time for war, once again

God of War II came out mere months after the PlayStation 3's launch in late 2006, but it was made with the PS2 in mind. Before Santa Monica rebooted God of War in 2018, players were enjoying a very different breed of god-killing and avenging in the 2000s. What the studio did with God of War in 2005 received a healthy upgrade with the sequel, garnering the same universal acclaim yet again.

Whether you consider the bloody, brutal combat or the bombastic boss fights, God of War II never lets you rest. The gorgeous level design and satisfying revenge plot make for a breathtaking action-adventure title that pushed the series to new heights, and was undoubtedly one of 2007's highlights.

God of War II

God of War II tasks players with bringing Kratos' revenge to fruition as he bludgeons his way through gods and other mythological beings. The game received universal acclaim for its excellent combat, story, and overall setting.

$19 at Amazon

9 BioShock (PC, Xbox 360)

Shockingly masterful and creative

Before Columbia, there was Rapture. Bioshock and its sequels proved to be some of the most impactful games of all time. Set in the underwater city of Rapture, the first game gave players tools they'd never seen before — injectable plasmids that gave them superhuman powers, environmental gameplay that forced them to think on their toes, and atmospheric storytelling that grappled with complex themes rarely tackled in such a fashion.

The year 2007 was home to several landmark new IPs, but BioShock was surely one that had a far bigger and more emotional impact than most.

BioShock

BioShock is a unique first-person shooter whose influence cannot be overstated. Its groundbreaking storytelling and gameplay, immersive setting, and exploration of complex themes inspired many more landmark games.

$20 at Steam $20 at Epic Games Store

8 Assassin's Creed (PS3, Xbox 360)

The birth of the creed

The original Assassin's Creed came out in 2007, telling master assassin Altair's story and setting the stage for one of the best-selling video game franchises of all time. Set in the Holy Land in the 11th century, the game allows players to inhabit a rich, historically accurate world filled with deadly assassins and templars locked in an everlasting struggle for peace through contrasting ideals. Accessing genetic memories through the Animus hooked players like never before.

Players were introduced to unique stealth mechanics, satisfying combat, and a solid narrative revolving around magical artifacts and even a modern-day element. The game had its issues, and later entries received more acclaim, but it was more than successful in achieving its goal — establishing a new IP based on rich lore, innovative gameplay, and painstakingly created sandboxes.

5 Images
5 Images
Assassin's Creed

The franchise that defined Ubisoft started in 2007 with Assassin's Creed and Altair's quest for truth in the 12th-century Holy Land. The historically accurate setting, action and stealth gameplay, and a rich narrative gave players a game they didn't know they needed.

$20 at Steam $20 at Epic Games Store

7 Super Mario Galaxy (Nintendo Wii)

Conquering the galaxy

In a year filled with Assassin's Creed, BioShock, Crysis, and Uncharted, Super Mario Galaxy still shines bright. Nintendo certainly knows how to make engaging games, and Super Mario Galaxy remains one of the company's greatest games ever. Being Mario's first 3D adventure in 5 years, and the first Mario game on the Nintendo Wii, expectations ran high, but the game proved to be an instant classic. It's one of the only games to have a 97 Metacritic score.

Jam-packed with more gameplay innovations than you can remember, a revamped platforming approach, and endlessly creative challenges for the titular plumber to overcome, Super Mario Galaxy was so influential that it received a rare sequel in the form of Super Mario Galaxy 2. You might not be able to pick up a new copy today, but it's still possible to buy a pre-owned copy from eBay or Amazon.

Super Mario Galaxy

Arguably one of the best Nintendo games ever released, and the greatest Mario game ever, Super Mario Galaxy took the plumber in new directions, each of which made the game an instant classic. Making great use of the Wii's motion controls and new gameplay mechanics, it was one of 2007's best gaming moments.

$37 at Amazon

6 Team Fortress 2 (PC, PS3, Xbox 360)

Another win for Team Valve

Team Fortress 2 was a Half-Life mod in Team Fortress Classic, that Valve later turned into a full-fledged multiplayer shooter with genre-defining class-based gameplay. The company chose a light-hearted aesthetic that gave the characters unique personalities, and allowed players to focus on their tactics and abilities.

The game went free-to-play in 2011, relying on microtransactions for cosmetics and other upgrades, and it was rewarded for doing so. Multiple unique modes, maps, and weapons and constant free updates kept the game fresh and popular. It inspired many other greats of the genre, such as Overwatch, and remains one of Valve's most successful ventures.

Team Fortress 2

Valve's Team Fortress 2 remains one of the most popular action multiplayer games of all time. Featuring distinct classes with unique abilities and tactics, the game laid the groundwork for many of today's iconic shooters. The fun aesthetic of the game belies its complex and thoughtful gameplay.

Free at Steam

5 Halo 3 (Xbox 360)

Home run

While Halo 3 came to PC only in 2020 as part of Halo: The Master Chief Collection, it enjoyed years worth of stellar online multiplayer. While the game is regarded as one of the greatest of all time, the single-player campaign was nothing to write home about. Nonetheless, what Bungie managed to achieve with the game's multiplayer component was downright phenomenal.

The variety in the game's maps, excellent matchmaking, and endless customizable modes created one of the biggest and most active multiplayer communities ever seen. With Halo: The Master Chief Collection, the game's multiplayer still lives on, easily being one of the most popular options among all the installments.

Halo 3

Halo 3 was Xbox 360's best entry in 2007, and is widely regarded as one of the best games of all time. The bulk of that acclaim is due to its multiplayer maps, modes, and matchmaking, with the story being just okay.

$10 at Steam $10 at Xbox Games Store

4 Portal (PC, PS3, Xbox 360)

A portal to a glorious past

The 2000s saw Valve hit one home run after another, and Portal became one of its freshest new games. Praised for its innovative gameplay mechanics and ability to force players to truly absorb their surroundings, Portal was a puzzle-solving game that always felt like much more than that. Underscored by a healthy dose of hilarity and referencing the Half-Life mythos, the game proved more successful than anyone could have imagined.

Portal demands thoughtful exploration and problem-solving, but the rewards are well worth it. As you open portals through space and pull yourself and objects through them, the short but sweet narrative engulfs you whole, leaving you satisfied yet wanting for more. Portal 2, a sequel, came out in 2011 with new gameplay additions and a co-op mode, but the original is unique in its own way.

Portal

Valve was on a magnificent high in the 2000s, and Portal was one of its most innovative new games. Arming players with a gun that can open portals through space, the game tasks players with solving puzzles in breathtaking new ways.

$10 at Steam

3 The Witcher (PC, Mac)

Creating a lasting fantasy world

The famed monster slayer and one of the most charismatic characters in video gaming, Geralt of Rivia began his journey in 2007's The Witcher. Developed by a small team at CD Projekt Red and based on a book series, no one expected the game to transform into the media behemoth that it is today.

The game not only presented a rich, dark fantasy world for players to get immersed in, but also delivered varied combat and gameplay. Besides, the story was one of the game's strongest points, which remained true across the trilogy. The Witcher is clearly one of the best entries of 2007, a year already stacked with gems.

The Witcher

CD Projekt Red's The Witcher gave birth to an iconic RPG series that has become the benchmark of the genre. Based on a Polish book series, the game sees Geralt of Rivia fight monsters and make morally grey choices in a dark fantasy world.

$10 at Steam $10 at GOG

2 Mass Effect (Xbox 360)

Massive effect on the RPG genre

If you asked anyone to name the greatest RPGs of all time, the Mass Effect series would be in the top 10 every time. The original game created and successfully implemented ideas that can be seen in games even today. A rich story-based RPG with a huge emphasis on player choice combined with cinematic presentation and high-definition visuals, Mass Effect became the standard for the new era of RPGs.

The characters were memorable, the stakes were high, and the world was a delight to explore. It also polarized the dialogue wheel that is a staple of RPGs today. While the two sequels were arguably better in terms of combat and overall execution, they owe everything to the 2007 original.

Mass Effect

Mass Effect gave rise to one of the greatest sci-fi RPG series of all time, and the ideas conceived by the first game had a lasting impact on the genre. Tasked with preventing the extinction of all life, you play as Commander Shepard, exploring a vast galaxy with rich lore and memorable characters.

$30 at Steam $30 at Epic Games Store

1 Half-Life 2: Episode Two (PC, PS3, Xbox 360)

Its half-life seems endless

For some, Valve's Half-Life 2 alone being on this list would be enough to crown 2007 as the greatest year for video games. While the base game came out in 2004, two sequels followed in 2006 and 2007 respectively. The latter was the last mainline entry until the VR prequel, Half-Life: Alyx, came out in 2020. While Episode Three was also planned for 2007, it never saw the light of day.

Half-Life 2, along with its prequel, did more for the video game medium than perhaps any other title. Regarded as one of the greatest games of all time, it allowed players to experience a deep sci-fi narrative, highly innovative gameplay, realistic physics, and advanced character models and animations. With whispers of Half-Life 3 again resurfacing, it's a great time to revisit this game-changing title.

Half-Life 2: Episode 2

Half-Life® 2: Episode Two is the second in a trilogy of new games created by Valve that extends the award-winning and best-selling Half-Life® adventure.

As Dr. Gordon Freeman, you were last seen exiting City 17 with Alyx Vance as the Citadel erupted amidst a storm of unknown proportions. In Episode Two, you must battle and race against Combine forces as you traverse the White Forest to deliver a crucial information packet stolen from the Citadel to an enclave of fellow resistance scientists.

Episode Two extends the award-winning Half-Life gameplay with unique weapons, vehicles, and newly-spawned creatures.

 

$10 at Steam

The 2000s gave us countless gems

2007 might be at the very top, but the entire decade from 2000 to 2010 was a treasure trove of iconic titles. Whether you're a fan of Deus Ex, GTA: San Andreas, Dead Space, Assassin's Creed II, Fallout 3, The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion, Silent Hill 2, Batman: Arkham Asylum, Metal Gear Solid 3: Snake Eater, or Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic, they all came out in the 2000s.
